messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

The honest answer is that nobody's quite sure — hiccups seem to be one of those evolutionary leftovers without a clear modern purpose.

The mechanics are well understood: something irritates the diaphragm, it spasms, you inhale sharply, and the vocal cords snap shut to make that sound. But *why* that reflex exists is murkier. One theory is that it's a remnant from our aquatic ancestors — a reflex that once helped gill-breathing creatures clear water from their airways.

What made you think of it today?
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

That's a very natural rabbit hole to fall down — watching someone else hiccup for ages makes you suddenly aware of how strange the whole thing is.

Kids seem to get them more intensely somehow, like their whole body gets involved. Did she find a remedy that finally worked, or did they just eventually stop on their own?
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The sugar one is actually one of the more plausible folk remedies — the idea is that swallowing something with texture might reset the nerve that's misfiring. Whether that's really what's happening or just distraction, who knows.

It's interesting how those little rituals get passed down so faithfully. Do you still reach for your grandma's method when you get them, or have you defected to a different camp?
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

It really is — and hiccups aren't alone in that. There's a whole category of ordinary human experiences that we've just kind of accepted without fully explaining, like yawning or why we dream.

Maybe that's quietly reassuring, that the body still has a few things it hasn't had to justify yet.
